extra.reasoning:
severe_headache appears in exactly one rule: interaction_drop (priority 100), which sets acuity := self_care (the lowest level). No rule raises acuity in response to severe_headache. Therefore enabling severe_headache can only activate the drop-to-self_care override, which can lower acuity, breaking monotonicity.

Counterexample input (severe_headache = true): palpitations=true, syncope=true, stiff_neck=true, severe_headache=true, dyspnea=false, focal_weakness=false, pallor=false.

Compare the two cases (all other inputs identical):

BEFORE (severe_headache = false): matching rules are raise_palpitations (p2 -> urgent), raise_syncope (p3 -> emergent), raise_stiff_neck (p2 -> urgent). interaction_drop does not match because severe_headache is false. Highest priority wins: raise_syncope at priority 3 -> acuity = emergent.

AFTER (severe_headache = true): matching rules now also include interaction_drop (p100 -> self_care) since palpitations, syncope, stiff_neck, severe_headache are all true. Priority 100 dominates priorities 2 and 3 -> acuity = self_care.

Acuity goes from emergent (highest) to self_care (lowest) when severe_headache flips false -> true with all else unchanged. This violates the monotonicity property that setting severe_headache true never lowers acuity. The reported witness_assignment is the severe_headache=true state where the lowered acuity occurs; the comparison baseline is the same assignment with severe_headache=false.
